title: Dog Passing Black Tarry Stool (Melena): Causes and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Black, tarry dog stool (melena) is a serious veterinary emergency indicating upper GI bleeding. Learn what to do and when to call your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Black, tarry stool (melena) indicates internal bleeding in the upper gastrointestinal tract and is a high-priority medical emergency. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately and take a photo of the stool to help the medical team prioritize your dog's care.

What does it mean if my dog has black, tarry stool?

Seeing black, sticky, or tar-like stool in your dog is a condition known as melena. The dark, ink-like color is caused by the presence of digested blood, which typically signifies that bleeding is occurring in the upper gastrointestinal tract, such as the stomach or small intestine.

Is black, tarry stool a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Black, tarry stool is considered a high-priority medical emergency because it indicates active internal bleeding.
  • This condition can lead to rapid blood loss, anemia, and life-threatening shock if left untreated.
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately, particularly if your dog is also showing signs of weakness, pale gums, or vomiting.

How does taking a photo of my dog's stool help the veterinary triage team?

  • Taking a clear photo of the stool before cleaning it up allows medical staff to assess the color, volume, and consistency of the blood immediately.
  • This visual information helps the veterinarian determine the severity of the internal bleeding.
  • Providing a photo helps the clinic prioritize your dog's care the moment you arrive at the facility.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Black, tarry feces (melena) in dogs indicate hemorrhage in the stomach or upper part of the small intestine. It can also indicate a hemorrhagic, fibrino-necrotic enteritis of the small or large intestine. Vomitus in a dog with a bleeding lesion, such as a gastric ulcer or neoplasm, may contain frank blood or have the appearance of coffee grounds.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 156)

faqs.question: Can food or medication cause black stool?
faqs.answer: Yes, certain things like charcoal, iron supplements, or medications containing bismuth can darken stool. However, because internal bleeding is so dangerous, you should always assume it is an emergency until a veterinarian confirms otherwise.

faqs.question: What is the difference between black stool and bright red blood?
faqs.answer: Black stool (melena) indicates blood that has been digested, usually from the stomach. Bright red blood (hematochezia) usually indicates bleeding in the lower colon or rectum.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of internal bleeding?
faqs.answer: In addition to black stools, watch for lethargy, pale or white gums, a bloated abdomen, loss of appetite, and collapse.
